articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 15, 1914. Occasioned by a newly formed North Carolina society, Bolivar traces Tar Heel influence on Black Philadelphia: John Chavis (first Black man ordained to the Presbyterian ministry, 1801) induced John Gloucester to come from Lexington, Ky. and found the First African Presbyterian Church in 1807; Frederick A. Hinton of N.C. was in the forefront of the anti-slavery movement of 1831 and a charter member of the Philadelphia Library Company of Colored Persons (1832), whose minutes Bolivar read through — colleagues included William Whipper, Robert Purvis, James Forten Jr., James Needham, Junius C. Morrell, John C. Bowers, James McC. Crummill, Rev. William Douglass, Robert B. Gordon, Jacob C. White and Robert Douglass. Rev. William J. Alston (N.C.) succeeded William Douglass as rector of St. Thomas' and fought for street-car rights by boarding cars and resisting expulsion; Mary J. Patterson (N.C.), Oberlin classmate of Fanny M. Jackson, came with her in 1865 to teach at the Institute for Colored Youth.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Nov. 8, 1913. On Quakers and abolition, keyed to Joseph Kite's 1843 pamphlet 'A brief statement of the testimony of the religious Society of Friends against slavery and the slave trade': Pastorius and the Germantown protest of 1688, the yearly meeting protest of 1696, Chester Quakers 1711-14, William Penn's manumission clause, John Woolman's book, Benjamin Lay, and Anthony Benezet (converted to Quakerism 1750, died 1784, his funeral attended by thousands including three or four hundred colored people). Quaker training of skilled artisans explains why the 'renaissance period' of 1787 found people of color so well equipped; by the Free African Society's founding in 1787 free people outnumbered slaves two to one.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 15, 1912. On the A.M.E. Church fixing Philadelphia for its 1916 centennial General Conference: the 1816 founding conference at which Richard Allen was made first bishop (Absalom Jones laying hands in consecration; Daniel Coker a strong competitor), with Jacob Tapsico, Clayton Durham, James Champion and Thomas Webster of Philadelphia mainly instrumental. Bolivar recalls St. Thomas' opening July 1794, Allen–Jones friendship, the 1793 yellow-fever service of Allen, Jones and William Gray (per Allen's memoirs and Dr. Benjamin Rush), the early Black insurance company, the 3,000-man Independence Square muster of 1812 promoted by Allen, Jones and Forten, and the first secular convention (1817); he urges a centennial historical exhibit, noting two early Allen portraits owned by Miss Sallie Wilkins of Philadelphia and Prof. Kealing of Kansas.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 25, 1912. History of beneficial societies, prompted by a Sons of St. Thomas banquet: the Free African Society (1787) as fountainhead; Newport and Charleston ('Brown Fellowship', c. 1796) parallels; the Sons of St. Thomas born 1813, promoted by Rev. Russell Parrott (assistant to Absalom Jones), disbanded and immediately reorganized 1857; scores of local societies named for Richard Allen, Clayton Durham, Benezet, Joseph Cox and J.P.B. Eddy; a Female Benevolent Society constitution of 1818; the Old Men's Beneficial Society; the Quaker City Beneficial Society (1884, conceived by Frank J. R. Jones); and the Fraternal Society (c. 1861), with T. J. Dutrieuille and E. G. Baxter surviving founders. Cites Willson's 'Colored Society in Philadelphia' (1841) on such bodies as mutual aid and cover for anti-slavery concert.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 13, 1914. Second A.M.E. centennial installment, drawing on Bishop Wayman's 'Recollections': the first conference at Sixth and Lombard (Allen, Daniel Coker, Jacob Tapsico, Clayton Durham); circuit-riding hardships, bonfire-and-hot-brick heating, no evening services before gas light. Philadelphia sustainers of the Allen era: Rev. Morris Brown, Shadrach Bassett, Walter Proctor, Willis Nazrey, Richard Williams, Joseph Cox, John Boggs, Edward Waters, William Moore, and among women Jarena Lee and Mary Lewton. Morris Brown, a Charlestonian, fled here as a suspect after the Denmark Vesey insurrection of 1822 and died 1849 after ~20 years as bishop. Daniel A. Payne's stand for an educated ministry — backed by George Hogarth, David Ware, Stephen Smith and Walter Proctor, joined in the early forties by Rev. William T. Catto — produced the Church Magazine in 1841. J. A. Shorter and John M. Brown came to Philadelphia as trade apprentices and rose to bishoprics.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 25, 1914. Colored pioneers of the Philadelphia Post Office: after the Fifteenth Amendment, Postmaster Gen. H. Bingham appointed Rev. James Underdue the first colored clerk (in payment for campaign oratory), then Daniel P. Adger as organizer; early additions Alfred S. Cassey (5th Ward), Edward Lewis (8th), Louis N. Bedford (23rd); Robert M. Adger (sponsored by Amos R. Little) served until his death; earliest letter carriers Jacob R. Ballard and S. Clifton Moseley; Pliny I. Locke's unsurpassed examination average won him the Money Order Department, with James H. Williams, John S. Durham, and John Dorsey (Registration Bureau). From the first appointee in 1870 to some 200 colored employees in 1914, with a warning that the new Wilson-era requirement to state race on applications portends elimination.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 11, 1913. On the Tribune's move from Sansom ('Printers' Row') to Sixteenth Street, Bolivar — a contributor since the first issue — recounts the paper's 1884 founding by Christopher J. Perry with encouragement from the late Andrew F. Stevens; contemporary 'penny-a-liners' Daniel P. Adger, H. Price Williams, Alexander Davis and Samuel C. Williams; George Gardner's Sentinel; and the Tribune staff over its Sansom Street years: John S. Durham, Randolph C. Lewis, Jack Handy, Hockley Smiley, Samuel B. Hart, Thomas Seldom, compositor-reporter Charlie Armstrong, John W. Harris, Jimmie Morgan, Ernest Mahand, proof-reader Bertha Perry (later Mrs. Paul G. Prayer), Will Fisher ('Hot Shots From the Bat'), Misses Sprague and Freeman, and Joseph Bassett Jr. Notes the still-unsolved mystery of the 'Bessie to Emma' letters and the Tribune as pioneer of Black illustrated journalism with its own photographic plant.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 5, 1912. A 'back to the farm' plea grounded in the history of Black farm ownership around Philadelphia: the Groves brothers' productive farms in Delaware County; James Forten's farm just above Girard College nearly a hundred years ago; Cyrus Bustill's farm at Bustleton; Robert Purvis's two country seats at Byberry aggregating over 250 acres and his brother Joseph Purvis's large Bucks County place; Mrs. Rachel Lloyd's acreage on the Bristol Pike (later John W. Price's country seat); Joseph Cassey's very large farm on the Neshaminy high ground; Col. John McKee's three or four adjoining places at Croydon; Peyton DeWitt's place still a nursery; and the Walls and Draper family farms in Chester County. Bolivar contrasts this with the post-1890 migration from Maryland and Virginia farm districts into precarious city labor. Includes portraits of the late Robert Purvis and Col. John McKee.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 27, 1912. The election that made William S. Stokeley mayor and the assassination of Octavius V. Catto (October 1871): in 'Bill' McMullin's Fourth Ward, Isaac Chase was hacked to death in his Emeline Street home and Bolden and Gordon shot down on Eighth Street even before Catto's murder, with 'Reddy' Dever and McDevitt spurred on by 'Dad' Cunningham, 'Jack' McKeown and 'Billy' Barnes; members of Crucifixion Church and Morris Brown Mission were in jeopardy. Stevedore Edward Hall knocked McMullin down rather than surrender his vote and won the bully's public respect. Closes with the old Eighth Ward's worthy families on and above Locust Street — Major Layton, Samuel Bustill, John W. Price, the Maybughs, Alexanders, Waters, Stevens, VanBrakles, Hazzards, Morgans, Ballards, Manluffs, Moores, Dickersons, Dillons, Joneses, Accoos, Harmons, Wests, Baptists, Mintons, Edwards, Dorseys, Evanses and Lewises — property owners there for a century.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 11, 1914. A rebuttal of Howard Odum's Columbia University sociological report on Black Philadelphia as slum-biased slander, answered with evidence of the 'unobtrusive, yet purposeful element': the James Forten anecdote of a distinguished white visitor's French letter translated on the spot by one of Forten's daughters (over eighty years ago), and — prompted by an Elijah Hodges letter — the programme of the Olive Cricket Club's library-fund entertainment of May 22, 1860 at the New Masonic Hall, Eleventh below Pine, by 'mere school boys': Master John R. Kennedy directing the orchestra and playing violin solo, Francis Wood's anniversary address, William H. Swan's Cato's Soliloquy, Frisby Stevens's comic number, James P. Clay and Edward Johnson leading a chorus, Edward E. Thomas's paper 'Great Deeds', James H. Francis addressing the audience, and a drama with James H. Francis, Charles S. Hayes, Henry Price, Fielding Butler, James H. Williams and William H. Mills.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 7, 1912. Music-teaching lineages, prompted by the death (June 10) of Mrs. Rebecca Wright, born White: Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield, the 'Black Swan', lived with Rev. Jeremiah Asher on Rodman above Tenth and ran a little conservatory whose pupils included Alice Green (her first concert supporter, died young), Lucy Davidson (afterward Mrs. Lucy G. Adger, who filled the Swan's place as singer, promoter and manager), Rachel Freeman (stage name Kate Lanier), William W. Morris, Rebecca Wright and Josephine C. Davis. Joseph G. Anderson of Lombard below Eighth, bugle and cornet player in Frank Johnson's band and the most accomplished musician of his generation, taught scores (Hamilton A. Moore, later of an English governmental band; Gen. William H. Patterson), trained the band of the first colored regiment recruited at Camp William Penn, was mustered in as band leader, and was succeeded in music by his two sons.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 22, 1912. A commencement-season sketch of early Black school history: Anthony Benezet's Quaker school equipping the men of 1787; Rev. Absalom Jones's private school lifting the curriculum, supported by the newly money-able just prior to 1800; a second Quaker school opened c. 1802 taught by an unnamed colored woman; Bird's school (now the Forten) opened 1823, its pupils walking miles through snow and hoodlums since children were excluded from public conveyances; family tutors including Margaretta Forten and Edward Johnson; Sarah M. Douglass's school on Arch near Third; early women teachers Amelia Bogle, Margaretta Barclay and Sarah Beulah; Ishmael Locke's school at Salter's Hall, which was the Institute for Colored Youth on Barclay Street; Adam Driver uniting preaching to pedagogy; Hester Powers (Palmer) paid from city funds; the Quaker Stokes at the Raspberry Alley school; W. M. Fuller's school in Israel Methodist Church, Fifth and Gaskill; Jacob C. White leaving the I.C.Y. to head the Vaux School during the war; first Frankford teacher James LeCount Jr. (then John H. Davis); Germantown pioneers Rebecca J. and J. William Cole, followed by Ada V. LeCount and Miranda C. Venning. With portraits of the late Thomas J. Bowers, David B. Bowser and Hans Shadd.

articles.headline: Pencil Pusher Points
articles.author: William Carl Bolivar
articles.summary: Philadelphia Tribune, May 11, 1912. Philadelphia as a center of anti-slavery and Black letters: Lay and Benezet arguing in print before the Revolution; Franklin, Paine, Woolman, Channing; Banneker's almanac published here soon after the Free African Society's founding; Absalom Jones's 1804 printed argument against colonization; Phillis Wheatley's poems; Russell Parrott taking up the cudgels in 1814; David Walker's Appeal (1830); Richard Allen's autobiography issued shortly before his death, clearing up much back to 1787; 'The Moral Reformer' periodical written and issued in Philadelphia in the 1830s, with fugitive efforts by Misses Sarah Forten and Sarah M. Douglass and notably Junius C. Morrell and William Whipper; Dr. Joseph Willson's 'Colored Society in Philadelphia' (1841, ed. 1,000 copies, only seven now traceable); and the bound reports of the Anti-Slavery (Colored Men's) Conventions of 1831-1836.

events.name: Philadelphia Library Company of Colored Persons founded
events.description: The first Black literary society, the Philadelphia Library Company (of Colored Persons), formed in 1832 with Frederick A. Hinton of North Carolina a charter member. Bolivar read all its minutes and found Hinton's written addresses and debates equal to or surpassing those of colleagues William Whipper, Robert Purvis, James Forten Jr., James Needham, Junius C. Morrell, John C. Bowers, James McC. Crummill, Rev. William Douglass, Robert B. Gordon, Jacob C. White and Robert Douglass (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 15, 1914).

events.name: William J. Alston succeeds William Douglass as rector of St. Thomas'
events.location: St. Thomas' African Episcopal Church, Philadelphia
events.description: Rev. William J. Alston of North Carolina succeeded Rev. William Douglass after his death as rector of St. Thomas' Church; his tenure covered most of the war period, and in the fight for street-car rights he boarded cars and resisted expulsion at risk of bodily harm (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Aug. 15, 1914).

events.name: First A.M.E. General Conference; Richard Allen consecrated first bishop
events.description: The African Methodist Episcopal Church was organized in Philadelphia in 1816 and Richard Allen made its first bishop, with Rev. Absalom Jones — ordained by Bishop White — placing his hands on Allen's head in consecration. The men mainly instrumental in shaping the connection were Jacob Tapsico, Clayton Durham, James Champion and Thomas Webster of Philadelphia, with eleven others; Rev. Daniel Coker of Maryland was a strong competitor for the bishopric (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 15, 1912).

events.name: Yellow fever epidemic relief led by Allen, Jones and Gray
events.description: During the yellow fever epidemic, Richard Allen, Absalom Jones and William Gray managed relief work among the sick and dying, as recorded in Allen's autobiography and the works of Dr. Benjamin Rush (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 15, 1912).

events.name: Sons of St. Thomas beneficial society founded
events.description: The Sons of St. Thomas was born in 1813, its promoter Rev. Russell Parrott, a young man greatly beloved and assistant to Rev. Absalom Jones — the name a compliment to the church, though the society had no direct connection with it. It disbanded in 1857 as a formality to divide its money and was immediately reorganized, continuing to the present (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, May 25, 1912).

events.name: Fraternal Society founded
events.description: The Fraternal Society, mainly the fruition of a thought by a Charlestonian for co-operation in time of sickness and death, was formed about fifty-one years before 1912, with T. J. Dutrieuille and E. G. Baxter the surviving founders (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, May 25, 1912).

events.name: Morris Brown flees Charleston for Philadelphia after the Vesey insurrection
events.description: Rev. Morris Brown, a Charlestonian, came to Philadelphia just after the Denmark Vesey insurrection of 1822, fleeing as a suspect and through many difficulties; he became contemporary bishop with Richard Allen and died in 1849 after holding the office nearly twenty years (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 13, 1914).

events.name: Daniel A. Payne's educated-ministry campaign and the A.M.E. Church Magazine
events.description: Rev. Daniel A. Payne took a firm stand for an educated ministry on his advent in Philadelphia; the opposition nearly drove him from the church, but he was backed by Revs. George Hogarth, David Ware, Stephen Smith and Walter Proctor, joined in the early forties by the fairly well-educated Charlestonian Rev. William T. Catto. One outgrowth was the Church Magazine in 1841 (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 13, 1914).

events.name: First Black appointments to the Philadelphia Post Office
events.location: Philadelphia Post Office, Fifth and Chestnut streets
events.description: After the Fifteenth Amendment, Postmaster General H. Bingham signalized his advent by appointing Rev. James Underdue to a clerkship — the first recognition of the new voting element, in payment for oratorical services in the first campaign — followed by organizer Daniel P. Adger, then Alfred S. Cassey (5th Ward), Edward Lewis (8th) and Louis N. Bedford (23rd), the pioneers of the Philadelphia Post Office service (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 25, 1914).

events.name: Philadelphia Tribune founded by Christopher J. Perry
events.description: The Philadelphia Tribune was 'tremblingly launched' in 1884 by Christopher J. Perry — promoter, owner, editor, news gatherer and distributor all in one — who came from the late Andrew F. Stevens full of encouragement over his project. Bolivar was part and parcel of its first issue and conducted the Pencil Pusher department for twenty years (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jan. 11, 1913).

events.name: Black-owned farms and country seats around Philadelphia
events.location: Delaware, Bucks, Chester counties and environs of Philadelphia
events.description: Bolivar catalogues the Black farm owners of the region: the Groves brothers of Delaware County; James Forten's farm just above Girard College nearly a hundred years before 1912; Cyrus Bustill's farm at Bustleton; Robert Purvis's two Byberry country seats of over 250 acres and Joseph Purvis's large Bucks County place; Mrs. Rachel Lloyd's Bristol Pike acreage, later John W. Price's country seat; Joseph Cassey's very large farm on the Neshaminy; Col. John McKee's three or four adjoining places at Croydon; Peyton DeWitt's place near the old Cassey estate; and the Walls and Draper family farms of Chester County (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Oct. 5, 1912).
attendees: owner of two Byberry country seats

events.name: Election riots and assassination of Octavius V. Catto
events.description: At the election that made William S. Stokeley mayor, 'Bill' McMullin's Fourth Ward cohorts turned intimidation into murder: Isaac Chase was hacked to pieces in his home on Emeline Street, Bolden and Gordon were shot down in cold blood on Eighth Street even before the murder of Octavius V. Catto, with 'Reddy' Dever and McDevitt spurred to riot by 'Dad' Cunningham, 'Jack' McKeown and 'Billy' Barnes. Stevedore Edward Hall, ordered from the polls by McMullin, knocked the bully down and held his ground, winning McMullin's public handshake as 'the first black man not to show fear in my presence' (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 27, 1912).

events.name: Olive Cricket Club library-fund entertainment
events.location: New Masonic Hall, Eleventh below Pine, Philadelphia
events.description: The Olive Cricket Club, 'a number of mere school boys', gave a literary and musical entertainment to raise funds for a library: Master John R. Kennedy directed an orchestra and played a violin solo; Francis Wood delivered the anniversary address and sang 'Loved Thee Dearly Loved Thee'; William H. Swan gave Cato's Soliloquy and a comic song; Frisby Stevens sang a comic number; James P. Clay and Edward Johnson led a chorus; Edward E. Thomas read an original paper, 'Great Deeds'; James H. Francis addressed the audience; and a drama featured Francis, Charles S. Hayes, Henry Price, Fielding Butler, James H. Williams and William H. Mills (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jul. 11, 1914).

events.name: Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield's vocal conservatory at Jeremiah Asher's
events.location: North side of Rodman above Tenth Street, Philadelphia
events.description: Elizabeth Taylor Greenfield, the 'Black Swan', lived with Rev. Jeremiah Asher and used the parlors as training rooms for piano and vocal lessons between concert tours. Pupils included Alice Green (her first concert supporter), Lucy Davidson (afterward Mrs. Lucy G. Adger), Rachel Freeman (stage name Kate Lanier), William W. Morris, Rebecca Wright née White, and Josephine C. Davis (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 7, 1912).

events.name: Joseph G. Anderson trains the band of the first colored regiment at Camp William Penn
events.description: When the Civil War progressed to include colored soldiers, Joseph G. Anderson — bugle and cornet player of Frank Johnson's band and the most accomplished musician of his generation — trained a band in the first regiment recruited and drilled at Camp William Penn, became its leader, was mustered in as such, and served at the front; his two sons also served and made music their profession (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Sep. 7, 1912).

events.name: Bird's school (later the James Forten school) opens
events.description: Bird's school, now the Forten, opened in 1823 as the first public school for colored children; pupils walked long distances through rigid winters and past lawless volunteer firemen, since children were excluded from public conveyances (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 22, 1912).

events.name: Ishmael Locke's school at Salter's Hall becomes the Institute for Colored Youth
events.description: Ishmael Locke kept school at Salter's Hall for quite a spell, and it was the Institute for Colored Youth on Barclay Street; among other early schools were Sarah M. Douglass's on Arch near Third, Adam Driver's (uniting preaching to pedagogy), and those of women teachers Amelia Bogle, Margaretta Barclay and Sarah Beulah, with Hester Powers (Palmer) paid from city funds (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, Jun. 22, 1912).

events.name: 'The Moral Reformer' periodical issued in Philadelphia
events.description: A periodical entitled 'The Moral Reformer' was written and issued in Philadelphia in the 1830s — a forerunner of the Anglo-African Magazine (1859-60), A.M.E. Review and Crisis — with fugitive literary efforts of that period published by Misses Sarah Forten and Sarah M. Douglass and notably Junius C. Morrell and William Whipper (W. C. Bolivar, Philadelphia Tribune, May 11, 1912).
